name: AI Safety and Governance
tagline: Processes, evaluation, and constraints to reduce harm from deployed models
problem:
Powerful models can cause harm through mistakes, bias, misuse, or overconfidence. Treating safety as an afterthought creates brittle systems and public backlash.

Governance solves the coordination problem: define risk thresholds, audit trails, and deployment constraints so AI remains a tool rather than a hazard multiplier.

The remaining problem for AI Safety and Governance is operational reliability. A reader needs more than a name and a historical note: they need to know what failure this capability prevents, what resources it consumes, what signs show that it is failing, and when it is safer to stop than to improvise. This entry therefore treats ai safety and governance as a teachable system with inputs, inspection points, records, and limits rather than as a single trick.
overview:
Safety includes technical work (evaluation, robustness, calibration, security) and organizational work (policy, reviews, incident response). Governance means decisions are documented and reversible.

For The Codex, this node matters because 'AI from scratch' is not only math and compute. It is also deciding what you will not deploy, and how you will detect failure early.

In collapse recovery, ai safety and governance must be repairable with local materials, teachable to more than one person, and documented well enough that the method survives injury, migration, or leadership change. In a space-settlement context, the same capability must account for closed-loop resources, contamination control, spare-part scarcity, power budgets, and the cost of mistakes in a sealed habitat. The practical minimum is a controlled trial, written checks, safe storage, and review before scale-up.

principles.name: Risk management
principles.explanation: Identify hazards, likelihood, and impact; choose mitigations with accountable owners.

principles.name: Defense in depth
principles.explanation: Layer dataset curation, evaluation, constraints, and monitoring.

principles.name: Auditability
principles.explanation: Keep logs, provenance, and versioning so failures can be investigated.

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what AI Safety and Governance is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

components.name: Input control point
components.function: Checks materials, tools, information, and environmental conditions before work starts
components.position: At receiving, storage, or setup area
components.madeFrom: Labels, inspection notes, gauges, samples, and quarantine bins
components.criticalNote: Unknown inputs should be isolated until checked.

rawMaterials.name: Paper and writing media
rawMaterials.purpose: Record calculations, assumptions, and experimental outcomes
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Digital notes plus printed backups; radiation-safe storage for critical references.

rawMaterials.name: Measurement tools
rawMaterials.purpose: Quantify reality so models are not guesswork
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Prefer multi-use instruments; maintain calibration artifacts and procedures.

rawMaterials.name: Time and attention
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Training time is a hard constraint; plan curricula and drills early.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Deliberate practice, peer review, and documentation.

rawMaterials.name: Incident logs and feedback
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Same requirement; treat incident response as life-support for software systems.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Triage, root-cause analysis, and corrective actions.

rawMaterials.name: Electric power
rawMaterials.purpose: Energy for computation, storage, and networking
earthLocations: Electrical grids globally; generators in remote sites
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Solar + batteries is the simplest early-space pathway; plan for tight energy budgets and thermal rejection.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Stable voltage regulation, grounding, and basic power-quality control.

buildSteps.title: Define unacceptable outcomes
buildSteps.description: Write what harms must be prevented; map to measurable tests.
buildSteps.warningNote: If you cannot define harm, you cannot prevent it.

buildSteps.title: Build evaluation gates
buildSteps.description: Require passing safety tests before deployment; document exceptions.

buildSteps.title: Deploy with constraints
buildSteps.description: Rate limits, access controls, and monitoring; keep rollback plans.

buildSteps.title: Run incident response drills
buildSteps.warningNote: First incident is not the time to invent a process.

buildSteps.title: Define the local purpose and stop conditions
buildSteps.description: Write down why AI Safety and Governance is needed, who is responsible, what success looks like, and which conditions require stopping or escalation.
buildSteps.warningNote: Do not start a life-critical or infrastructure-critical use without clear stop conditions.

history.event: Safety concerns rise with model scale and deployment.
Risk frameworks and red-teaming become standard.

impact:
Safety and governance determine whether AI accelerates civilization or becomes a destabilizing force. They also enable long-term maintainability through documentation and audit trails.

In the Codex tree, this node is a prerequisite to responsible deployment of large-model systems in either Earth recovery or space settlement scenarios.
